sales.title:
Otho, Denarius, 69, Rome, Silver, NGC, AU★ 5/5-5/5, RIC:10

Otho, Denarius, 69, Rome, Silver, NGC, AU★ 5/5-5/5, RIC:10
Bare head of Otho, right., Securitas, draped, standing left, holding wreath in right hand and sceptre in left hand., Graded NGC AU★ Strike : 5/5 Surface : 5/5.

The reign of Otto was short - barely three months - and only gold and silver coins were minted. His coinage was therefore rare and sought-after. Our example is in superb condition, still freshly minted and featuring a remarkable portrait! This denarius is all the more beautiful for its magnificent cabinet patina!

Otto became Roman emperor in January 69 after assassinating his predecessor Galba. Along with Vitellius and Vespasian, he was part of the so-called “Year of the Four Emperors”. This was a short period of civil war following Nero's suicide, during which three emperors succeeded one another. The fourth one, Vespasian, closed this period of instability by installing the Flavian dynasty. The first emperor, Galba, overthrew Nero with the support of Otto, whom he eventually betrayed by adopting Piso. Otho had them both assassinated and became emperor. Initially very close to Nero, their relationship deteriorated. One of the main reasons for this was Poppea, first Otho's wife, then Nero's second wife by 63. Despite his accession to the title of emperor, he was contested in some parts of the Empire, who preferred Vitellius. Vitellius became emperor following his predecessor's suicide shortly after his defeat at Bedriacum., IMP OTHO CAESAR AVG TR P, SECVRITAS P R
DENOMINATION:Denarius
YEAR:69
COIN CONDITION:AU★ 5/5-5/5
MINT NAME:Rome
COMPOSITION:Silver
DIAMETER:18
CERTIFICATION:NGC
CERTIFICATION NUMBER:6639688-002
RIC:10
EMPIRE, KINGDOM, OR CIVILIZATION:Ancient Rome, Roman Empire (27 BC – AD 476)
WEIGHT (G):3.64

If this product is to be delivered outside of France, it is subject to export authorizations from the Ministry of Culture. This procedure requires a processing time of 2 to 5 months from the date of purchase.


Otho, Denier, 69, Rome, Argent, NGC, AU★ 5/5-5/5, RIC:10
Tête nue d'Othon à droite., Securitas, drapée, debout à gauche, tenant une couronne de la main droite et un sceptre de la main gauche., Graded NGC AU★ Strike : 5/5 Surface : 5/5.

Le règne d'Othon est court - à peine trois mois -, seules des monnaies d'or et d'argent sont frappées. Son monnayage est donc rare et recherché. Notre exemplaire est dans un superbe état de conservation, présente encore sa fraicheur de frappe et arbore un remarquable portrait ! Ce denier est d'autant plus beau qu'il présente une magnifique patine de médailler !
Othon devient empereur romain en janvier 69 après avoir assassiné son prédécesseur Galba. Avec Vitellius et Vespasien, il fait parti de ce qu'on appelle "l'Année des quatre empereurs'. Il s'agit d'une courte période de guerre civile suivant le suicide de Néron pendant laquelle se succèdent trois empereurs. Le quatrième, Vespasien, clôt cette période d'instabilité en installant la dynastie flavienne. Le premier empereur, Galba, renverse Néron avec le soutien d'Othon, qu'il finit néanmoins par trahir en adoptant Pison. Othon les fait assassiner et devient empereur. D'abord très proche de Néron, leur relation se dégrade. L'une des raisons principales est Poppée, d'abord épouse d'Othon puis deuxième femme de Néron dès 63. Malgré son accession au titre d'empereur, il est contesté dans certaines parties de l'Empire qui lui préfère Vitellius. Celui-ci devient empereur à la suite du suicide de son prédécesseur survenu peu après sa défaite à Bedriacum., IMP OTHO CAESAR AVG TR P, SECVRITAS P R
VALEUR FACIALE:Denier
ANNÉE:69
QUALITÉ DE LA MONNAIE:AU★ 5/5-5/5
ATELIER:Rome
MÉTAL:Argent
DIAMÈTRE:18
CERTIFICATION:NGC
NUMÉRO DE CERTIFICATION:6639688-002
RIC:10
EMPIRE, ROYAUME OU CIVILISATION:Rome antique, empire romain (27 av. J.-C - 476 apr. J.-C)
POIDS (GR):3.64

sales.title:
Nero, as Augustus (AD 54-68). AR denarius (18mm, 3.26 gm, 7h). NGC Choice VF S 5/5 - 5/5. Rome, ca. AD 64-68. NERO CAESAR-AVGVSTVS, laureate head of Nero right / IVPPITER-CVSTOS, Jupiter enthroned left, thunderbolt in right hand, grounded scepter in left. RIC I 53. Pearl-like toning. From the Werner Collection. Nero, born Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus in 37 AD, ascended to the Roman imperial throne in 54 AD, initiating a tumultuous reign that both captivated and horrified contemporaries. Initially perceived as a youthful and promising ruler, Nero's early years were marked by relative stability. However, his later years were characterized by a series of erratic and tyrannical acts. Nero's infatuation with the arts and his lavish spending on grandiose construction projects, such as the Domus Aurea, strained the Roman treasury. His ruthless suppression of the Pisonian conspiracy and persecution of Christians tarnished his legacy further. The Great Fire of Rome in 64 AD, though often attributed to Nero, remains a subject of historical debate. Ultimately, Nero's reign culminated in a revolt by the Roman Senate, leading to his suicide in 68 AD, marking the end of the Julio-Claudian dynasty and leaving a complex legacy as both a patron of the arts and a symbol of tyranny in Roman history. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice

sales.title:
Ancients
Macrinus (AD 217-218). AR denarius (19mm, 3.68 gm, 12h). NGC AU 5/5 - 4/5. Rome, December AD 217. IMP C M OPEL SEV-MACRINVS AVG, laureate, draped, and cuirassed bust of Macrinus right, seen from behind, with older features and long beard / P M TR P I-I C-OS P P, Annona standing facing, head left, grain ears in outstretched right hand over filled modius at feet before, cornucopia cradled in left arm. RIC IV.II 26. Crisp details on gunmetal dies. Ex Distinctive Coins, private sale with old dealer's tag included. Macrinus was born in Mauritania around AD 165. His family was upper middle-class, allowing him to receive an education that propelled him to high ranks as a bureaucrat during the reign of Septimius Severus. Caracalla appointed Macrinus as praetorian prefect, a position of significant power, second only to the emperor. In AD 216, while accompanying Caracalla on a campaign against the Parthians, Macrinus learned of a letter implicating him in a plot against the emperor. Acting swiftly, he arranged for Caracalla to be assassinated by one of his bodyguards. Macrinus proclaimed his innocence and convinced the army to declare him emperor on April 11, AD 217. Though the Senate in Rome was pleased to be rid of the unpopular Caracalla, they viewed Macrinus with skepticism but were willing to give him a chance. Hoping to disengage from the campaign and return to Rome, Macrinus sought a peace agreement with the Parthians. However, sensing weakness, they massed their forces, forcing Macrinus to concede large bribes and reparations. To the Roman soldiers, this seemed like a defeat, and Macrinus further alienated them by revoking the generous pay increase Caracalla had granted. Discontent grew, and Caracalla's influential aunt, Julia Maesa, bribed the Roman garrison of Emesa in Syria to proclaim her 13-year-old grandson, Elagabalus, as emperor on May 15, AD 218. Initially dismissive of the revolt, Macrinus eventually marshaled his loyal legions to meet the rebels near Antioch on June 8. Despite his troops nearing victory, Macrinus lost his resolve and fled. His demoralized soldiers switched sides, declaring their support for Elagabalus. Disguised as a courier, Macrinus attempted to escape across Asia Minor but was captured in Chalcedon and executed. His son met a similar fate while en route to exile in Parthia. HID05401242017 © 2025 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
